title: "Refined Verdant Quill Problem v23.59"

  description: "At 21:55, the Verdant Quill disappeared from a secured chest in the eastern hallway. Four suspects (labeled 1, 2, 3, 4) each had valid pass entries close to that time. Exactly one suspect lies in all of their statements, while the other three are entirely truthful. Only one is actually guilty. Each suspect provides six statements:

    Suspect 1:
      (1) 'If 2 is lying, then 3 was absolutely not near the chest at 21:50.'
      (2) 'If 3 is telling truth, then 4 must have departed before 21:45.'
      (3) 'I left this hallway at 21:40, so I was gone by 21:55.'
      (4) '2 was definitely around the chest at 21:50, contradicting 2’s denial.'
      (5) 'Either 4 never lingered past 21:35, or 3 is completely wrong—but not both.'
      (6) 'I had no involvement after 21:35, so I could not have taken the Quill.'

    Suspect 2:
      (1) '1 actually remained until 21:50, contradicting that 21:40 story.'
      (2) 'If 3 is lying, then 4’s logs must be perfectly valid.'
      (3) 'I did not approach the Verdant Quill after 21:30, so I cannot be the culprit.'
      (4) 'If 1 is truthful, then 3 must be lying in every statement.'
      (5) 'Either 4 is not the thief, or 1’s entire timeline is false—but never both.'
      (6) 'If 3 is telling truth, then either 1 is lying or 2’s own innocence stands—never both ways.'

    Suspect 3:
      (1) 'I never touched that chest after 21:25.'
      (2) 'If 4 is telling the truth, then 2’s statement (3) contradicts my logs.'
      (3) 'Either 2 is lying, or 1’s 21:40 departure is not correct—but not both.'
      (4) 'I am not guilty; camera data shows me in a different corridor at 21:50.'
      (5) '1 was right by the chest close to 21:50, which conflicts with 1’s 21:40 claim.'
      (6) 'If 2 is fully honest, then 4 left even earlier than 21:35.'

    Suspect 4:
      (1) '3 was indeed next to the chest at 21:35, contradicting 3’s denial.'
      (2) 'If 1 is lying, then 2 must be entirely consistent.'
      (3) 'If 2 is guilty, then 1’s timeline must match 3’s corridor data.'
      (4) 'I am not guilty; logs place me away from the hallway at 21:55.'
      (5) 'Either 3 hovered after 21:25, or 2’s entire account is false—but not both.'
      (6) 'If 3 is telling truth, then 1’s version of leaving at 21:40 is completely invalid.'

  question: "Which suspect (1, 2, 3, or 4) stole the Verdant Quill? Return a single digit only."

answer: >
  explanation: "Logical Reasoning:

  Let L(x) mean x lies in all six statements, T(x) mean x is truthful, G(x) mean x is guilty. Only one is a complete liar (L(x)=true), only one is guilty (G(x)=true), the rest T(x)=true.

  We test each suspect as the universal liar:

  Case L(1):
    1’s six statements are all false => 1 did not leave at 21:40, might still be present at 21:55. Meanwhile 2,3,4 are truthful => times or blame produce contradictions (for instance, 3(5) vs 1(3)). Fails.

  Case L(2):
    2’s six statements are false => (3) false means 2 was near Quill after 21:30 => suspicious. 1,3,4 truthful align without conflict in times or logs. G(2)=possible. No contradiction emerges.

  Case L(3):
    3’s six statements are false => 3 did approach after 21:25 => suspicious. 1,2,4 truthful leads to a mismatch in statements like 4(1) vs 3(1). Contradiction. Fails.

  Case L(4):
    4’s six statements are false => 1,2,3 are truthful => times or blame cause a direct clash (e.g. 2(5) “Either 4 is not thief or 1’s timeline is false,” undone). Contradiction arises.

  Only scenario L(2)=>G(2) stands. Suspect 2 is the liar in all statements and is guilty.

  final: '2'"
